title: What is inheritance
instruction:
You have a `User` class. Now you need `AdminUser` — just like `User` but with admin powers.

Copying the whole class would be a disaster to maintain. Inheritance lets a child class **inherit** everything from a parent and only add what is new.

```python
class AdminUser(User):  # AdminUser inherits from User
```

Run this. `AdminUser` gets all of `User`'s attributes for free.
successMessage: AdminUser inherited greet() and the __init__ from User without copying a single line. Only delete_user() is new. Change User and AdminUser gets the update automatically.

title: super() — extend the parent
instruction:
`super()` calls the parent class's method.

Use it in `__init__` to run the parent's setup code, then add your own:

```python
class AdminUser(User):
    def __init__(self, name, email, permissions):
        super().__init__(name, email)  # run User's __init__
        self.permissions = permissions  # then add ours
```

Add a proper `__init__` to `AdminUser` using `super()`.

codeChecks.message: Call super().__init__() to run User's setup.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: super().__init__() delegates to the parent. AdminUser gets all of User's setup plus its own extra field. This is the clean way to extend classes.

title: Method overriding
instruction:
A child class can override a parent method by defining a method with the same name.

Python uses the child's version instead of the parent's.

Override the `greet()` method in `AdminUser` to include their role.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Python finds greet() in AdminUser first — the parent's version is shadowed. Override only the methods that need different behaviour; inherit the rest.

title: isinstance() and issubclass()
instruction:
`isinstance(obj, Class)` checks if an object is an instance of a class — or a subclass of it.

`issubclass(Child, Parent)` checks class relationships.

Run this and understand the results.
starter:
class Vehicle:
    pass

class Car(Vehicle):
    pass

class Truck(Vehicle):
    pass

my_car = Car()

print(isinstance(my_car, Car))      # True
print(isinstance(my_car, Vehicle))  # True — Car is a Vehicle
print(isinstance(my_car, Truck))    # False
print(issubclass(Car, Vehicle))     # True
print(issubclass(Truck, Car))       # False
successMessage: isinstance(my_car, Vehicle) is True because Car inherits from Vehicle. This is how you write code that works for any Vehicle — cars, trucks, bikes — all at once.

title: Multiple inheritance
instruction:
Python allows a class to inherit from multiple parents:

```python
class FlyingCar(Car, Airplane):
    pass
```

The MRO (Method Resolution Order) determines which parent's method to use when both have the same name. Check it with `ClassName.__mro__`.

Run this.
successMessage: Flyable's move() wins — Python follows MRO left to right. Multiple inheritance is powerful but use it carefully. Prefer composition over deep inheritance hierarchies.

title: Build an animal hierarchy
instruction:
No starter code.

Build a class hierarchy:
- `Animal(name, sound)` — base class with a `speak()` method that prints `f"{name} says {sound}"`
- `Dog(name)` — inherits from Animal, always has sound "Woof"
- `Cat(name)` — inherits from Animal, always has sound "Meow"

Create one Dog and one Cat, call speak() on both.

codeChecks.message: Call super().__init__() in Dog and Cat.

successMessage: Dog and Cat inherit speak() from Animal — no code duplication. Both handle their specific sound in __init__ via super(). Chapter 14 done.
